content.text: Empirically discovered power-law relationships predict how
as three training resources grow: the number of model parameters
—let practitioners estimate a model's final performance from small proxy experiments, and choose the compute-optimal split between model size and data volume.

Scaling laws describe how the cross-entropy loss of a language model falls as a power law in each of the three primary training resources. The relationship was first quantified by Kaplan et al. (2020) for GPT-style causal language models and later revised—and in practice overturned—by Hoffmann et al. (2022), whose
model demonstrated that earlier large models were systematically undertrained.
The central equation from the Chinchilla work expresses loss as the sum of an irreducible term and two power-law decay terms:
is the irreducible entropy of the data—the floor loss no model can beat;
are the exponents governing how quickly loss falls with each resource.
A complementary identity links the three resources for dense-attention transformers. Running a forward pass through a model of size
FLOPs (accounting for both forward and backward passes during training). This identity is the bridge between the loss function above and decisions about how to spend a compute budget.
Training frontier models costs tens of millions of dollars. A scaling law lets a team
predict the outcome of a full training run from a handful of small proxy experiments
, avoiding expensive guesswork. Before Chinchilla, the dominant intuition—inherited from Kaplan et al.—was that model size (
) mattered far more than training tokens (

), leading to models like GPT-3 (175 B parameters, trained on 300 B tokens). The Chinchilla analysis showed that at a fixed compute budget those models were an order of magnitude underdata-trained. Chinchilla (70 B parameters, 1.4 T tokens) outperformed Gopher (280 B) at one-quarter the parameter count.
For agent and product builders who consume rather than train base models, scaling laws matter differently: they explain
why newer, seemingly smaller models often outperform older, larger ones
(more efficient data use), and they predict when additional fine-tuning data will yield diminishing returns.

distinction. A model trained compute-optimally for a single run may be larger than ideal for inference at scale. Smaller models trained on more data achieve the same quality at a fraction of the per-query cost—a trade-off that LLaMA 2 and LLaMA 3 exploited deliberately.
small proxy runs at 0.1–1% of the target compute budget can fit the law's coefficients, enabling accurate extrapolation.
comparing models on equal compute budgets (IsoFLOP curves) removes the confound of differing training token counts.
the decay exponents predict how much loss improvement remains with additional training data.
compute-optimal training prescribes smaller, more data-saturated models, which are cheaper to serve.
scaling exponents reveal whether bottlenecks are in model capacity, data quality, or compute budget.
scaling laws are curve fits on specific architectures and data distributions. They do not guarantee transfer to new model families, tokenizers, or corpora.
the law treats tokens as interchangeable. A trillion tokens of high-quality curated text and a trillion noisy web tokens give very different loss values—the law only characterises quantity.
power-law loss does not always predict task-specific accuracy. Some capabilities exhibit sharp phase transitions ("emergent abilities") that look discontinuous on log-log plots.
accurately fitting coefficients requires at least seven to ten proxy runs spanning an order of magnitude in
serving a larger compute-optimal model at billions of requests per day may be more expensive than serving a smaller model trained on more tokens.
coefficients shift when the tokenizer, sequence length, or attention mechanism changes. MoE architectures require a separate FLOP multiplier.
has a clean interpretation. The term
captures the model's capacity limitation: a finite network cannot represent all statistical structure in the data, and that gap shrinks as parameters increase. The term
captures the data limitation: even a model of infinite capacity would overfit to finite training data, and that gap shrinks as more tokens are seen. The constant
is the Bayes-optimal entropy of natural language—the irreducible uncertainty in predicting the next token given any preceding context.
Fitting the five parameters requires solving a nonlinear least-squares problem. Each proxy run provides one
triple. With roughly ten proxy runs spanning at least one order of magnitude in both
, a standard solver converges reliably to stable coefficients.

content.text: Think of training a model like running a
factory with a fixed energy budget

. You can invest that budget in two ways: hire more specialised workers (model parameters) or supply more raw material (training tokens). Hiring far more workers than you have material leaves most workers idle—parameters that never see enough varied examples to learn. Flooding the floor with material but too few workers means nothing gets processed efficiently—data the model cannot generalise from given limited capacity.
Scaling laws are the engineering formula that tells you the
optimal ratio of workers to material

for any energy budget. Before Chinchilla, labs were running enormously overstaffed factories. Chinchilla showed that roughly equal investment in workers and material—one parameter per training token—is near-optimal under the current loss model.
is the factory's physical noise floor: even with infinite workers and infinite material, some uncertainty about the next token is irreducible because human language is genuinely stochastic.
Scaling law analysis is most valuable in the following situations:
run five to ten proxy experiments spanning 1–2 orders of magnitude in compute, fit the law, then extrapolate to decide on
hold compute fixed and measure which architecture family achieves lower loss across IsoFLOP slices.
when the data pool is bounded (domain-specific corpora), the data term dominates. Scaling law fitting reveals the point of diminishing returns.
a team serving millions of requests per day can use scaling laws to find the smallest model that achieves a target quality, then overtrain it on extra tokens.
scaling-law-predicted loss is a principled baseline for deciding whether an open-weight checkpoint is efficient for its compute class.

to use scaling law analysis: for fine-tuning a pretrained model (the pretraining law does not cover fine-tuning dynamics), or when predicting performance on a narrow task that may exhibit emergence rather than smooth power-law improvement.
pairs spanning roughly two orders of magnitude in compute. For a 1e22-FLOP target, run proxies from 1e20 to 1e21.
Train proxies and record validation loss.
Measure loss on a held-out validation split at end-of-run for each proxy configuration. Use the same tokenizer and data mixture as the target run.
Use nonlinear least squares to estimate
Run one intermediate-scale confirmation experiment and verify that predicted loss matches observed loss within a few percent before committing to the full run.
The fitting code below implements steps 3 and 4:

import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit

# ── Chinchilla scaling law: L(N, D) = E + A/N^alpha + B/D^beta
# Fit power-law coefficients from (N, D, loss) triples gathered at small scale.

def scaling_loss(ND, log_E, log_A, alpha, log_B, beta):
    """Vectorised loss prediction.
    ND: array of shape (n, 2) with columns [N, D].
    """
    N, D = ND[:, 0], ND[:, 1]
    E = np.exp(log_E)
    A = np.exp(log_A)
    B = np.exp(log_B)
    return E + A / N**alpha + B / D**beta

def fit_scaling_law(experiments):
    """
    experiments: list of dicts with keys 'N', 'D', 'loss'
    Returns fitted parameters (E, A, alpha, B, beta).
    """
    ND = np.array([[e['N'], e['D']] for e in experiments])
    losses = np.array([e['loss'] for e in experiments])

    # Initial guess: Chinchilla paper values
    p0 = [np.log(1.69), np.log(406.4), 0.3394, np.log(410.7), 0.2849]

    popt, pcov = curve_fit(scaling_loss, ND, losses, p0=p0, maxfev=10000)
    log_E, log_A, alpha, log_B, beta = popt
    perr = np.sqrt(np.diag(pcov))
    return {
        'E': np.exp(log_E),     'E_err': perr[0],
        'A': np.exp(log_A),     'A_err': perr[1],
        'alpha': alpha,          'alpha_err': perr[3],
        'B': np.exp(log_B),     'B_err': perr[3],
        'beta': beta,            'beta_err': perr[4],
    }

# Example: seven small proxy runs spanning two orders of magnitude in compute
experiments = [
    {'N': 70e6,   'D': 1.4e9,  'loss': 2.10},
    {'N': 125e6,  'D': 2.5e9,  'loss': 1.95},
    {'N': 350e6,  'D': 7.0e9,  'loss': 1.78},
    {'N': 700e6,  'D': 14e9,   'loss': 1.66},
    {'N': 1.3e9,  'D': 26e9,   'loss': 1.57},
    {'N': 3.5e9,  'D': 70e9,   'loss': 1.47},
    {'N': 7.0e9,  'D': 140e9,  'loss': 1.40},
]

params = fit_scaling_law(experiments)
print("Fitted Chinchilla parameters:")
print(f"  E (irreducible) = {params['E']:.4f}")
print(f"  A = {params['A']:.2f},  alpha = {params['alpha']:.4f}")
print(f"  B = {params['B']:.2f},  beta  = {params['beta']:.4f}")
Computing optimal N and D for a given compute budget

import numpy as np

# ── Compute-Optimal Allocation (Chinchilla Rule) ────────────────────────────
# For a dense transformer: C ≈ 6 * N * D  (forward + backward FLOPs)
# Chinchilla optimal: N* ≈ D*  (equal parameter-to-token ratio)

def chinchilla_optimal(C_flops: float, a=0.50, b=0.50, G=1.0):
    """
    Given a compute budget in FLOPs, return the optimal model size and
    number of training tokens under the Chinchilla parameterisation.

    Hoffmann et al. 2022 fitted:
        N* = G * (C / 6)^a
        D* = G^{-1} * (C / 6)^b
    where G ≈ 1, a ≈ 0.50, b ≈ 0.50 (roughly equal exponents).
    """
    base = C_flops / 6
    N_opt = G * base**a
    D_opt = (1.0 / G) * base**b
    return N_opt, D_opt

def predict_loss(N, D, E=1.69, A=406.4, alpha=0.3394, B=410.7, beta=0.2849):
    """Chinchilla loss prediction for given N and D."""
    return E + A / N**alpha + B / D**beta

# ── Plan training runs for three compute budgets ────────────────────────────
budgets_petaflops = [1e3, 1e4, 1e5]   # 1e3 = 1 petaFLOP

print(f"{'Budget (PF)':>12}  {'N* (params)':>14}  {'D* (tokens)':>14}  "
      f"{'N*/D*':>8}  {'L pred':>8}")
for C_pf in budgets_petaflops:
    C = C_pf * 1e15
    N_opt, D_opt = chinchilla_optimal(C)
    ratio = N_opt / D_opt
    L = predict_loss(N_opt, D_opt)
    print(f"{C_pf:>12.0f}  {N_opt:>14.2e}  {D_opt:>14.2e}  "
          f"{ratio:>8.3f}  {L:>8.4f}")

# ── IsoFLOP slices: compare loss at fixed C while varying N ─────────────────
C_fixed = 1e22   # 1e22 FLOPs
N_values = np.logspace(8, 12, 50)
D_values = C_fixed / (6 * N_values)

losses = predict_loss(N_values, D_values)
best_idx = np.argmin(losses)
print(f"\nIsoFLOP minimum at C=1e22:")
print(f"  N* = {N_values[best_idx]:.2e}, D* = {D_values[best_idx]:.2e}, L* = {losses[best_idx]:.4f}")
Extrapolating loss and estimating required compute

import numpy as np

# ── Extrapolating Loss to Large Scale from Small Proxy Runs ─────────────────
# After fitting coefficients on small experiments, predict the loss a large
# model would achieve before committing to the full training run.

def predict_loss(N, D, E=1.69, A=406.4, alpha=0.3394, B=410.7, beta=0.2849):
    return E + A / N**alpha + B / D**beta

def estimate_required_compute(target_loss, E=1.69, A=406.4, alpha=0.3394,
                               B=410.7, beta=0.2849):
    """
    Binary-search for the minimum FLOPs budget that achieves target_loss
    under Chinchilla-optimal allocation.
    """
    lo, hi = 1e18, 1e26
    for _ in range(100):
        mid = (lo + hi) / 2
        N, D = (mid/6)**0.5, (mid/6)**0.5
        if predict_loss(N, D) < target_loss:
            hi = mid
        else:
            lo = mid
    return hi

# ── How much compute to reach target losses? ────────────────────────────────
target_losses = [2.0, 1.9, 1.8, 1.7]
print(f"{'Target L':>10}  {'Min C (PF)':>12}  {'N* (B params)':>14}  {'D* (GT)':>10}")
for tl in target_losses:
    C = estimate_required_compute(tl)
    N = (C/6)**0.5
    D = (C/6)**0.5
    print(f"{tl:>10.2f}  {C/1e15:>12.1f}  {N/1e9:>14.2f}  {D/1e9:>10.2f}")

# ── Data-constrained scenario: fixed D, vary N ──────────────────────────────
D_fixed = 1e12   # 1 trillion tokens
print(f"\nData-constrained regime (D = {D_fixed:.0e} tokens):")
print(f"{'N (params)':>12}  {'Loss':>8}  {'Compute (PF)':>14}")
for N in [1e9, 7e9, 13e9, 70e9, 405e9]:
    L = predict_loss(N, D_fixed)
    C = 6 * N * D_fixed / 1e15
    print(f"{N:>12.0e}  {L:>8.4f}  {C:>14.1f}")

The LLaMA series illustrates compute-optimal scaling in practice. LLaMA 1 (2023) trained a 7 B-parameter model on 1 T tokens and a 65 B model on 1.4 T tokens—ratios of approximately 143 and 22 tokens per parameter respectively. These were already far more data-saturated than GPT-3 (300 B tokens / 175 B parameters ≈ 1.7 tokens per parameter). LLaMA 2 pushed the 7 B model to 2 T tokens. LLaMA 3 extended this further, training 8 B and 70 B models on 15 T tokens, yielding roughly 1,875 and 214 tokens per parameter—well beyond the Chinchilla-optimal 1 : 1 ratio, deliberately so for deployment efficiency.

: once a model is released and must serve millions of users, inference cost dominates total cost of ownership. A smaller model trained on far more data than compute-optimality prescribes often achieves the same quality as a larger compute-optimal model at a fraction of the per-query cost. Scaling laws quantify exactly how much quality is sacrificed—or gained—when trading off model size against training budget under a deployment constraint.

Chinchilla itself demonstrated this at the research level: 70 B parameters trained on 1.4 T tokens outperformed Gopher's 280 B parameters trained on 300 B tokens across nearly all evaluated tasks, while using one-quarter the parameters and therefore approximately four times less memory at inference time.
Using Kaplan (2020) exponents instead of Chinchilla (2022) exponents.
. The Chinchilla re-analysis found higher exponents and that the optimal ratio is roughly 1 : 1. Using Kaplan exponents produces overparameterised, undertrained models.
A model trained to minimise loss per FLOP of training is not the same as the model that minimises cost per query at scale. For long-lived deployments, overtrain a smaller model.
Scaling laws fit on web-crawl data do not transfer unchanged to domain-specific corpora. Coefficients must be refitted per data distribution.
Fitting on too few proxy points.
(five parameters) on three or four proxy runs produces unstable extrapolations. Use at least seven to ten data points.
split, not the training loss. Training loss diverges from validation loss under data repetition or overfitting.
Over-relying on perplexity as a proxy for downstream task accuracy.
Power-law loss improvements sometimes yield sub-linear or non-monotonic accuracy improvements on structured tasks like code generation or mathematical reasoning.
Spend 1–5% of the total compute budget on small-scale experiments before committing to the final run.
Log (N, D, loss) at multiple checkpoints.
Each checkpoint gives an additional data point for fitting, increasing coefficient stability.
Compute residuals between predicted and observed loss on proxy runs. A mean absolute error above 2% suggests distribution shift or poor coverage.
Before the final run, do one validation at roughly 10% of the target compute, and confirm the extrapolated prediction matches observed loss within 1–2%.
The law assumes each token is seen once. If data is exhausted and training continues for multiple epochs, the effective
does not simply double—repetition raises validation loss.
Use IsoFLOP curves for architecture comparison.
for two architectures to identify which is more parameter-efficient.
Refitting coefficients when the data mixture changes gives the most accurate predictions.
The practical utility of scaling laws comes from
FLOPs, where the law's power-law character means relative fitting error stays roughly constant in log-space. The primary scaling bottleneck shifts depending on the regime:
are below the Chinchilla-optimal ratio. Increasing either improves loss approximately equally per FLOP.
the available data is exhausted at reasonable quality. The
term dominates; adding parameters yields diminishing returns without more data.
the data pool is vast but the model is small. The
term dominates; more tokens help little without increasing
. No architecture can surpass this bound on a fixed data distribution.

For mixture-of-experts (MoE) architectures, the FLOP-per-parameter multiplier changes: since only a fraction of parameters are active per token, separate scaling law fits are needed. Early MoE results (Mixtral, Deepseek-MoE) suggest MoE architectures achieve lower loss at the same active-parameter count, but their scaling exponents are not yet as well characterised as dense-model laws.
triples across proxy runs; the parallel coordinates view makes it easy to compare runs at different scales.
distributed training frameworks used to run proxy experiments and full training runs efficiently across hundreds of GPUs.
a minimal PyTorch implementation ideal for cheap proxy experiments on a single GPU before scaling to a cluster.
integrates scaling law callbacks that log loss at regular checkpoint intervals to facilitate power-law fitting.
the standard Python entry point for nonlinear least-squares fitting of the five-parameter Chinchilla model.
provides standardised task evaluations to complement perplexity-based scaling analysis with downstream task metrics.

# Minimal scaling-law experiment tracker
import json, pathlib

def log_run(run_dir: str, N: int, D: int, val_loss: float):
    """Append a (N, D, loss) triple to a JSONL log for later curve fitting."""
    path = pathlib.Path(run_dir) / "scaling_log.jsonl"
    with path.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps({"N": N, "D": D, "loss": val_loss}) + "\n")

def load_runs(run_dir: str):
    path = pathlib.Path(run_dir) / "scaling_log.jsonl"
    return [json.loads(line) for line in path.read_text().splitlines() if line]

# Usage
import tempfile, os
tmpdir = tempfile.mkdtemp()
log_run(tmpdir, N=70_000_000,   D=1_400_000_000, val_loss=2.10)
log_run(tmpdir, N=125_000_000,  D=2_500_000_000, val_loss=1.95)
log_run(tmpdir, N=350_000_000,  D=7_000_000_000, val_loss=1.78)

runs = load_runs(tmpdir)
print(f"Logged {len(runs)} proxy runs:")
for r in runs:
    print(f"  N={r['N']:.0e}  D={r['D']:.0e}  loss={r['loss']}")
Scale N much faster than D; models were overparameterised relative to data
Equal scaling of N and D; models were undertrained on data
Hyperparameter transfer across scales; complements scaling laws but does not replace them
Some tasks show phase transitions; perplexity-based power laws cannot predict them
Scaling laws for NLP (Ghorbani et al. 2021)
Confirmed Kaplan exponents on translation and summarisation; task-specific exponents vary
Scaling laws create several operational risks that grow with model scale:

the law encourages training on ever-larger datasets, which increases the attack surface. Adversarial documents mixed into the training corpus at even 1 in 10,000 can durably alter a large model's behavior, and that influence scales with
larger models trained on more data memorise training examples verbatim with higher probability. This creates privacy and copyright risks. The
term implies the model has processed each token deeply enough to store it.
scaling more data through the model increases the probability that evaluation benchmarks appear in training data, invalidating comparisons.

power-law fits extrapolate well within one to two orders of magnitude of the training distribution but can fail at extreme scales where emergent capabilities or dataset quality ceilings disrupt the smooth trend.
scaling law coefficients are sensitive to hyperparameter tuning (learning rate schedule, batch size). Running proxy experiments with different hyperparameters than the final run invalidates the fit.
Reliability best practice: maintain a held-out validation set that is
triple at multiple checkpoints per run to enable post-hoc audit of the fitted law.
Loss follows power laws in N, D, and C:
the optimal exponents are roughly equal (
), so model size and data volume should scale at similar rates.
; models trained before Chinchilla were an order of magnitude underdata-trained.
links the three resources for dense transformers; MoE architectures require a separate multiplier.
for inference-heavy deployments, train a smaller model on more tokens than compute-optimality prescribes.
Data quality is not captured by the law:
scaling laws quantify quantity; corpus quality must be controlled independently.
seven to ten small runs spanning two orders of magnitude in compute are enough to fit reliable coefficients.
Emergent abilities break the smooth power law:
some tasks exhibit sharp capability transitions that scaling laws cannot predict from perplexity alone.
